5 min read
Every entry point onto our email list should be allowed to do its
job of orienting a new subscriber or customer to our world and for
a relationship to have the time to take hold and be nurtured.
Subscribing to the VNL (after
an RBS/COS)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 12 --

Whether your business has a single-entry RBS, multiple RBSs, or
the addition of direct purchases (Customer Onboarding Series),
we need mechanisms in place that will “subscribe” someone to the
VNL loop at the appropriate time.
Making this happen can be a simple process — RBS VNL — or
made a little more challenging when there are multiple entry
points of varying lengths.
The best way to tackle this problem is to use custom �elds instead
of tags because, unlike tags that are binary (added or not), custom
�elds support “state changes” — meaning a single custom �eld
can hold any number of values that represents different states.
There is no one objective “best way” to set up your automations,
but we recommend that you use “best practices,” which will allow
for growth minus the potential headaches later.
Note: Even if you don’t yet have a VNL, we recommend you build
the logic into your automations from the get-go. That way, you’ll
be all set to introduce a VNL whenever it makes sense to you,
knowing the logic is already in place.
Step 1 — Create a Custom Field
The �rst thing we do is create a custom �eld vnl_status (you can
name this anything), which we’ll use to hold vales of Muted and
Subscribed , and any other state variation you need.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 12 --

Step 2 — Create ‘New Subscriber’ Automation
Now we create an automation that captures all our entry points,
free and/or paid. We have our RBS entry point (far left) and one
for each paid course in this example.
This represents every possible way someone can enter our email
world.
The next thing we need to do is check if a value already exists for
vnl_status . If this is a new subscriber, the custom �eld will be
blank. We now want to write Muted to the �eld, then end the
automation.
But if vnl_status is not blank, this means it’s either already set to
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 12 --

Muted or Subscribed , states we use elsewhere, so the automation
can also be allowed to end.
Step 3 — RBS Automation
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 12 --

The automation to control an RBS is simple. In this example, you
can see our ‘Emergent Marketing Series’ RBS. It’s triggered when
the RBS - Emergent Marketing Series (EMS) tag is added.
(Remember, at this point, as seen in Step 2, vnl_status is set to Muted .)
The subscriber is added to the RBS - Emergent Marketing Series
(EMS) sequence. Our sequence is seven emails long, at which
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 12 --

point the subscriber is unsubscribed from the sequence and
continues through the automation.
At this point we set the vnl_status custom �eld to Subscribed ,
indicating that the subscriber can now receive our VNL.
(Once set to Subscribed , the value of the custom �eld will not be
changed again UNLESS you decide you want to “mute”
subscribers from your VNL in certain circumstances. We’ll cover
this in a separate section.)
Step 4 — Create VNL Broadcast Segment
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 12 --

The example above is a segment that targets:
�. all subscribers , AND
�. has the custom �eld vnl_status set to Subscribed .
Meaning: every subscriber that relieves a VNL broadcast would
have completed our RBS (or any other RBS).
But what happens when if someone makes a purchase as their
�rst entry point into our email world and enters one of our COSs?
Let’s tackle that next.
Step 5 — COS Automations
The simple version of a Customer Onboarding Series would
behave no different to an RBS. First, the sequence runs, then when
�nished, writes Subscribed to the vnl_status custom �eld.
However, a typical COS is longer than an RBS/SOS. And in some
cases, a lot longer.
For example, ARM2 was a six-week COS. ARM3 had a four-week
COS. Some COSs that have ‘check-ins’ components could cause
the COS to run for months.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 12 --

For these longer COSs, we’ll most likely not want to wait for a COS
to “end” before we can write Subscribed to the vnl_status custom
�eld. Otherwise, these new customers would not receive our
VNLs for, well, many weeks or even months.
To get around this, we need automation that will write
Subscribed to the vnl_status custom �eld after a predetermined
delay.
Here’s an example for our Functions — COS — AoE automation:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 12 --

Let’s go through this automation.
First, we have a condition where we check the value of the
vnl_status custom �eld. If it already has the value Subscribed ,
the automation ends. In this case, the customer was already
“subscribed” from a previous entry point.
If the value is not Subscribed (if it’s set to Muted , which is the only
other value we use), a delay is started. In our example above,
Subscribed is written after a week. The length of the delay would
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 12 --

be something you need to decide based on your COS.
The question to ask is: When should a new customer be added to the
VNL broadcast segment?
The delay may be different depending on the length and intensity
of COS. But, again, only you can decide this based on factors
unique to you.
Note: This automation doesn’t kickoff off the COS sequence. We
have a separate automation, like this:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 12 --

You may wonder why we didn’t combine the two automations
into a single one. Wouldn’t that be easier and cleaner?
In the examples above, yes. But our live automations have a lot
more going on than this. We prefer to keep automations as simple
as possible, where they don’t try and do too much.
There’s nothing worse at staring down the barrel of an
automation that is doing too much. Automations can get unruly
quickly.
Please ask questions and leave comments below if you don’t
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 12 --

understand something.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 12 --
